instruction: Explain what LoRA is to a beginner.
output: LoRA is a way to fine-tune a large model by training small add-on weights instead of changing every original weight. This makes training much cheaper, faster, and easier to store.

instruction: Explain the difference between a base model and an instruct model.
output: A base model mainly continues text. An instruct model has already been trained to follow requests, so it is usually the safer starting point for a small instruction dataset.

instruction: Define QLoRA in simple terms.
output: QLoRA combines quantization with LoRA. The base model is loaded in a compressed form to save memory, while small LoRA adapters are trained on top. This lets you fine-tune larger models with less VRAM.

instruction: What is quantization and why is it useful in LLM work?
output: Quantization stores model weights in fewer bits, such as 4-bit instead of 16-bit. This reduces memory usage and often makes loading and inference cheaper, which is helpful when hardware is limited.

instruction: Explain what an eval set is and why it matters.
output: An eval set is a small group of examples held back from training. You use it to check whether the tuned model improved on unseen prompts instead of only memorizing the training set.

instruction: A teammate only has 40 carefully written examples. Why is starting from an instruct model usually safer than starting from a base model?
output: An instruct model already knows the basic pattern of following requests. With only 40 examples, the dataset can spend its limited signal teaching the task instead of first teaching the model how to respond like an assistant.

instruction: A beginner has 1500 training rows. What is the usual model choice rule of thumb?
output: With more than 1,000 rows, starting from the base model is often the default rule of thumb. The larger dataset gives the model enough signal to learn the target behavior more directly.

instruction: Explain why tiny, controlled experiments are better than ambitious first runs.
output: A tiny run is easier to debug. You can catch formatting mistakes, check whether the adapter helps, and avoid wasting GPU time before the workflow is stable.

instruction: A beginner asks whether their first fine-tuning weekend was a success. They only finished one short run. What would make that weekend count as a real win?
output: It counts as a real win if they proved the loop works end to end: a small clean dataset, one completed run, a saved adapter, and a clear base-versus-tuned check on held-out prompts.

instruction: Explain the practical role split between VPS, MacBook, and Modal in this project.
output: Use the VPS for orchestration, notes, repo work, and automation. Use the MacBook for local editing, notebooks, and light experiments. Use Modal for real GPU-backed training once the workflow is clear.

instruction: Why is dataset quality more important than dataset size for a first experiment?
output: High-quality examples teach the model the exact behavior you want. A small clean dataset is easier to inspect and debug, while a large noisy dataset can hide problems and produce unclear results.

instruction: What is an adapter in LoRA fine-tuning?
output: An adapter is the small set of trainable weights learned during LoRA fine-tuning. You keep the original model frozen and save the adapter separately so it can be reused or merged later.

instruction: Explain overfitting in one short paragraph.
output: Overfitting happens when the model learns the training examples too literally and does not generalize well to new prompts. In a small dataset project, signs include repetitive answers, memorized phrasing, and weak performance on the eval set.

instruction: What is a checkpoint during training?
output: A checkpoint is a saved snapshot of model state during training. It lets you resume training, inspect intermediate results, or compare earlier and later versions of the run.

instruction: Two weeks later, nobody remembers which prompts were used for training and which were held out for eval. Why is that a problem?
output: It breaks reproducibility and makes contamination harder to spot. If you cannot tell which prompts were held out, you cannot trust later comparisons or know whether the eval stayed separate from training.

instruction: What does reproducible mean in a small ML experiment?
output: Reproducible means someone can rerun the experiment and understand the same setup: dataset version, script, dependencies, hardware target, and expected outputs. It turns a one-off trial into reusable knowledge.

instruction: What is the purpose of a chat template in LLM fine-tuning?
output: A chat template converts messages such as system, user, and assistant turns into the exact text format expected by the model. Using the right template keeps training and inference consistent.

instruction: Why is Hugging Face authentication needed in this workflow?
output: Hugging Face authentication is often needed to download gated models, upload adapters, and manage datasets. Without a valid token, parts of the workflow may fail before training even starts.

instruction: Explain why the current VPS is not the place for the first real Unsloth training run.
output: The VPS has no NVIDIA GPU, so it is best used for planning, data prep, and orchestration. The first real Unsloth training run should happen on a compatible local machine or a remote GPU provider like Modal.

instruction: What is a good first evaluation habit after fine-tuning?
output: Always compare base and tuned answers on the same held-out prompts. That is the quickest way to see gains, regressions, or memorization.

instruction: Describe gradient accumulation like you are teaching a beginner.
output: Gradient accumulation lets you simulate a bigger batch size by processing several small batches before updating the model weights. It is useful when GPU memory is too limited for a large batch all at once.

instruction: Explain max sequence length in a practical way.
output: Max sequence length is the largest number of tokens the model sees at once during training or inference. Longer contexts can capture more information, but they also increase memory use and cost.

instruction: What does load_in_4bit do in a QLoRA setup?
output: load_in_4bit loads the base model in a 4-bit quantized form. This cuts memory usage enough to make fine-tuning practical on smaller GPUs while the LoRA adapters remain trainable.

instruction: When would you choose LoRA instead of QLoRA?
output: Choose LoRA when you have enough VRAM to keep the model in higher precision and you want a simpler setup. Choose QLoRA when memory is tight and you need quantization to fit the model.

instruction: What is the main risk of using a messy hand-made dataset?
output: The model will learn inconsistent targets. If answer style, correctness, or formatting varies too much, it becomes harder to tell whether failures come from the model or from the data.

instruction: Explain why a run log matters after training.
output: A run log preserves the setup, observations, and surprises from the experiment. Without it, later comparisons become fuzzy and you lose the main value of the first run: reusable learning.

instruction: Give a short explanation of why base-vs-tuned comparisons should use the same prompts.
output: Using the same held-out prompts isolates the model change. If the prompts also change, you cannot tell whether the difference came from the adapter or from different inputs.

instruction: Why should a beginner avoid RL at the start of this project?
output: RL adds more moving parts: rewards, rollouts, stability issues, and more compute. Starting with supervised fine-tuning first makes it easier to learn the basics without confusing failures.

instruction: What is a good default first remote model choice for this repo?
output: A strong default is unsloth/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct-bnb-4bit. It is small, already instruction-tuned, and well suited to a short first run whose goal is workflow validation.

instruction: Summarize the first experiment in three steps.
output: First, prepare a small clean dataset. Second, run one short fine-tuning job on a small instruct model. Third, compare base and tuned answers on held-out prompts and write a short debrief.

instruction: Explain why held-out prompts should cover both likely successes and likely failures.
output: A balanced eval set tells you more than a cherry-picked one. Success cases show where the adapter helps, while failure cases reveal blind spots, regressions, or overfitting.

instruction: What does SFT mean in this workflow?
output: SFT means supervised fine-tuning. You show the model examples of the desired prompt-and-answer behavior and optimize it to imitate that target behavior.

instruction: Why is a tiny dataset still useful even if it is not enough for a final model?
output: A tiny dataset is enough to validate the workflow. It helps confirm formatting, trainer settings, saving, and evaluation before you invest in bigger datasets or longer GPU runs.

instruction: Explain why wiki notes belong in the training workflow.
output: Wiki notes turn scattered observations into durable knowledge. They help you remember why a model was chosen, what the dataset tried to teach, and what changed after each experiment.

instruction: Give a short answer to: what problem does Unsloth solve for beginners?
output: Unsloth makes fine-tuning faster and more memory-efficient. It helps beginners run practical LoRA or QLoRA experiments with less hardware pain and less setup friction.

instruction: How should a beginner react if the first tuned outputs are only slightly better than baseline?
output: Treat that as useful feedback. Tighten the dataset, keep the eval prompts fixed, and change one variable before the next run.

instruction: Why is an instruct model usually the safer first choice when you only have a tiny dataset?
output: Because it already knows how to follow instructions. A tiny dataset can then focus on your task instead of teaching instruction-following from scratch.

instruction: Your teammate wants to celebrate after a tiny first run. What is the cautious version of success you would let them claim?
output: Claim that the workflow was validated, not that the model is solved. A cautious success means the data loaded, the short run finished, the adapter was saved, and the tuned model could be checked against the base model on held-out prompts.

instruction: A teammate accidentally copied the held-out eval prompts into the training file before a rerun. Why does that make the next score hard to trust?
output: Because the model can memorize those exact prompts during training. Then a better eval result may only show recall, not real transfer, so the run no longer tells you whether the adapter generalizes to new prompts.

instruction: How should a beginner explain Modal in one practical sentence for this project?
output: Modal is the remote GPU runner for this repo. It lets you launch reproducible training jobs from the VPS even though the VPS itself has no NVIDIA GPU.
